metadata.career_relevance: Generic methods are the foundation of Java's utility APIs. `Collections.sort()`, `Collections.min()`, `Arrays.asList()`, `Objects.requireNonNull()`, `Optional.of()` — all generic methods. After this topic, you understand and can write the same kind of type-safe utility code. In production, utility classes with generic static methods are extremely common: `CollectionUtils.firstOrDefault()`, `StringUtils.coalesce()`, `MapUtils.getOrCompute()` — all generic methods.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll write generic static utility methods with their own type parameters, understand how the compiler infers T from call-site arguments, write generic instance methods, distinguish when a method needs its own `<T>` vs inheriting from the class, and build a complete utility class of generic methods.

visual_aid.description: Show generic method anatomy: access modifier, `<T>` (highlighted as 'type param declaration'), return type T, method name, parameters using T. Arrow from `<T>` to each usage of T. Below: type inference diagram — List<String> passed as argument → compiler identifies T=String → return type becomes String → no cast needed. Side-by-side: method-level `<T>` vs class-level `<T>` — when each is appropriate. Bottom: Java library method signatures showing `<T>` placement in real APIs.
visual_aid.alt_text: Generic method anatomy, type inference flow, and method vs class type parameter placement

worked_example.scenario: Build a complete `Generics` utility class with type-safe static methods that mirror what professional Java utility libraries like Apache Commons or Google Guava provide. Each method solves a real daily programming problem generically.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Build `GenericUtils` with six utility methods:
1. `<T> T firstOrDefault(List<T>, T)` — first element or default
2. `<T> List<T> repeat(T, int)` — repeat value N times
3. `<A, B> List<Pair<A,B>> zip(List<A>, List<B>)` — zip two lists
4. `<T> T requireNonNull(T, String)` — like Objects.requireNonNull
5. `<T, R> List<R> transform(List<T>, Function<T, R>)` — like stream.map
6. `<T> Optional<T> findFirst(List<T>, Predicate<T>)` — like stream.filter.findFirst
things_to_notice: Each method declares `<T>` (or `<A, B>` or `<T, R>`) BEFORE its return type. This is the method-level type parameter — independent of any class-level type parameter.

Type inference in action: `repeat(0, 4)` — T is inferred as Integer from the argument `0`. `transform(names, String::toUpperCase)` — T=String from names (List<String>), R=String from String::toUpperCase return type.
zip uses TWO method-level type parameters A and B. The compiler infers A=String from names and B=Integer from scores.
transform is essentially what `stream().map()` does — but written as a plain generic method. The Stream API is built from generic methods like these.
findFirst is what `stream().filter().findFirst()` does. Both return Optional<T>.

requireNonNull returns T — this is the key! `String validated = requireNonNull(name, 'name')` — the compiler knows the return is String because T=String. No cast needed, same as Objects.requireNonNull.

industry_examples.use_case: Guava's `Iterables.getFirst(Iterable<T>, T)`, `Lists.transform(List<F>, Function<F,T>)`, `Collections2.filter(Collection<E>, Predicate<E>)` — all generic static methods. These look exactly like what you learn today. Every Java team uses or has built similar utility libraries.

Spring's `CollectionUtils.firstElement(List<T>)`, `ObjectUtils.getDisplayString(Object)`, `ReflectionUtils.invokeMethod(Method, Object)` — all generic methods. The entire Spring framework utility layer is built from generic methods that make code type-safe and reusable.

Every Java project has a `Utils.java` or `CollectionHelper.java` full of generic static methods: `getOrDefault(Map<K,V>, K, V)`, `firstMatch(List<T>, Predicate<T>)`, `mapToList(List<T>, Function<T,R>)`. These are what interns write in their first week and senior devs use daily.

applications.interview_relevance:
Generic method questions at mid-senior level:

- **'What is the difference between a generic class and a generic method?'** — Generic class: T on class, all instances share the type. Generic method: T on method, each call can have different T, inferred at call site.
- **'Write a generic method that swaps two elements in a list.'** → `static <T> void swap(List<T> list, int i, int j)` — uses the generic T without needing a generic class.
- **'Why does `Collections.emptyList()` return `List<T>` without explicitly passing a type?'** → Type inference from context. Assigning to `List<String>` tells the compiler T=String.
- **'When would you use `<T>` on a method vs on a class?'** → Method: when T is specific to one operation, especially static utilities. Class: when T defines what the object IS.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily generic method usage:

- Writing a utility: `static <T> T coalesce(T... values)` — return first non-null
- Wrapping a stream: `static <T, R> List<R> mapList(List<T> list, Function<T,R> fn)` — common helper
- Safe lookup: `static <K, V> V getOrThrow(Map<K,V> map, K key, String msg)` — typed getOrDefault with exception
- Test helpers: `static <T> T assertPresent(Optional<T> opt)` — unwrap or fail assertion

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Trying to use `<T>` from a static method as if it's from the class — or forgetting to declare `<T>` on the method entirely.
common_gaps.remediation: Static methods CANNOT use class-level type parameters (static context has no `this`). A generic static method must declare its own `<T>` before the return type: `static <T> T method(...)`, not just `static T method(...)`.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compile error: 'Cannot make a static reference to the non-static type T'

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Putting `<T>` after the method name or return type instead of before the return type.
common_gaps.remediation: `<T>` goes BEFORE the return type: `public static <T> List<T> method()` — not `public static List<T> <T> method()` or `public static List<T> method<T>()`.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Expecting `Collections.emptyList()` to always infer correctly — it can't when there's no context.
common_gaps.remediation: Type inference needs context. `var list = Collections.emptyList()` — can't infer, gets `List<Object>`. `List<String> list = Collections.emptyList()` — infers from declaration. `return Collections.emptyList()` where return type is `List<String>` — infers from return type.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Unexpected List<Object> instead of typed empty list

key_takeaways: Generic method: `<T>` declared BEFORE the return type — independent type parameter per method call
Static methods MUST declare their own `<T>` — cannot use class-level type parameters
Type inference: compiler deduces T from arguments — rarely need explicit `<String>method(...)` syntax
Generic instance methods: useful on non-generic classes for per-operation type flexibility
Multiple type params on a method: `<A, B> Pair<A,B> makePair(A a, B b)` — each independently inferred
When class has T and method adds R: `<R> Result<R> map(Function<T,R>)` — both in scope
Java library generic methods: Arrays.asList, Collections.sort/min/max, Optional.of, Objects.requireNonNull
Pattern: utility class (no class T) + generic static methods (each with own T) = Apache/Guava style
transform() is stream.map() as a plain method; findFirst() is stream.filter().findFirst()

self_check_questions.answer: Static methods cannot reference the class-level type parameter T — but there isn't even a class-level T here. The method needs to declare its OWN type parameter: `static <T> T process(T input) { return input; }`. The `<T>` before the return type is mandatory for generic static methods.

self_check_questions.question: What does the compiler infer for T in `repeat('x', 5)`?
self_check_questions.answer: T = Character. The first argument 'x' is a char literal auto-boxed to Character. So the method `<T> List<T> repeat(T value, int times)` returns `List<Character>`. Type inference happens from the first argument — Character.

A = String (from `List<String>` names), B = Integer (from `List<Integer>` scores). The compiler sees `zip(List<A>, List<B>)` and matches A to the element type of the first argument (String) and B to the element type of the second (Integer). Return type Pair<A,B> becomes Pair<String,Integer>, which matches the declared variable type.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: **Bounded Type Parameters** — restricting what types can be substituted for T. `<T extends Comparable<T>>` means T must implement Comparable. This unlocks methods like finding the maximum of any list, writing sort algorithms, and building type-safe numeric containers. Bounded type parameters are how `Collections.sort()` guarantees its list elements are sortable.
